text.title: Implementation Plan Writing Process
text.summary: The 11-phase process for writing an implementation plan, inputs and outputs per phase, document hierarchy, and wave analysis rules.

section.title: Document Hierarchy
section.p: The implementation plan sits between design and execution:

list.item: Spec (FR-X): WHAT and WHY — requirements and acceptance criteria.
Design (AD-X): HOW — architecture decisions, file structure, API contracts.
Implementation Plan (TASK-XXX outline): IN WHAT ORDER — task breakdown, waves, token budgets.
TASK_*.md files (executor input): WHAT EXACTLY — atomic work units with AC and file lists.

rule.statement: Write the implementation plan only after design.md has Accepted status. Writing the plan on a Draft design wastes tokens when requirements shift.
rule.rationale: Implementation plans derived from Draft designs must be rewritten when the design changes. The cost of rewriting a plan is higher than the cost of waiting for design approval.

rule.statement: Execute all 11 phases in order. Do not skip prerequisites check (Phase 2) or risk assessment (Phase 9) — these are the phases most commonly omitted under time pressure.
rule.rationale: Skipped prerequisites cause execution blockers. Skipped risk assessment means the executor encounters surprises that require mid-execution plan changes, which are expensive.

list.item: Phase 1: Load SDD context — read constitution.md, spec.md (extract FR-X), design.md (extract AD-X), features/done/ for patterns.
Phase 2: Prerequisites check — external services, database schema, no circular dependencies, all ADs documented.
Phase 3: WBS — list all CREATE/MODIFY/DELETE files, group into logical work units, apply 8-80 rule (not too big, not too small).
Phase 4: Dependency graph — map each task to its dependencies, verify no circular deps, identify critical path.
Phase 5: Wave analysis — group independent tasks (Wave 1), tasks depending on Wave 1 (Wave 2), continue; define checkpoint criteria per wave.
Phase 6: Phase definition — logical groupings above waves (e.g., Phase 1: Infrastructure, Phase 2: Core Logic).
Phase 7: Task format — fill TASK v2.0 format for Wave 1 only; later waves created after execution.
Phase 8: Critical path analysis — mark longest dependency chain explicitly.
Phase 9: Risk assessment — identify at least 3 risks with mitigation and contingency per risk.
Phase 10: Testing strategy — unit, integration, E2E test requirements; quality gates per wave.
Phase 11: Rollout strategy — deployment approach, data migrations, rollback trigger conditions.

rule.statement: Wave 1 must contain only tasks with zero dependencies. Agents can run them in parallel. If Wave 1 has no parallel-safe tasks, the dependency graph has a problem — review it.
rule.rationale: A Wave 1 with serial dependencies means the plan is not exploiting available parallelism. Review the dependency graph to identify incorrectly declared FS dependencies that should be concurrent.

rule.statement: Define explicit checkpoint criteria between waves. The executor must verify all Wave N tasks pass before creating Wave N+1 task files.
rule.rationale: Without checkpoint criteria, a Wave 1 failure is not detected until Wave 2 executes and produces wrong results. Checkpoints enforce the gate before the cost of Wave 2 is incurred.
